title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Managing Diabetes While Coping with Mental Health Challenges
Living with diabetes requires daily attention in all aspects of life, from monitoring blood sugar levels to planning meals, taking medications, and staying physically active. Sometimes, when stress, anxiety, or low mood enter the picture, these simple tasks can feel heavier. Many people don’t realise how closely the mind and blood sugar are linked. When your mental health dips, your motivation, routine, and self-care often dip with it, making diabetes more challenging to manage. And when diabetes feels overwhelming, it can, in turn, affect your emotional well-being. It becomes a challenging loop that can be broken with the proper support. Let’s know about the mental health challenges associated with diabetes and how to overcome them.
Why Mental Well-Being Matters in Diabetes Management?
Let’s first understand the different types of diabetes to know how they affect the routine and emotional balance, along with blood sugar levels:
Type 1 diabetes happens when the immune system destroys insulin-producing cells. Since the body can’t produce insulin, lifelong injections or an insulin pump are needed.
Type 2 diabetes develops when the body becomes resistant to the effects of insulin. It’s usually linked to lifestyle and is managed with diet changes, exercise, medicines, and regular monitoring.
Both forms require daily decisions, discipline, and stress control, which directly tie into mental health. The recent survey done by the International Diabetes Federation also brought surprising results into view:
86% of diabetics report depression, anxiety, or emotional distress.
Women are more affected: 90% of diabetic women say their condition has impacted their mental well-being, as against 84% of men.
85% got frustrated with the everyday diabetes care routine, and 73% of them paused their medication or treatment due to this mental fatigue.
Why Diabetes Often Leads to Stress?
Managing diabetes is constant, and here’s why many people feel overwhelmed while handling this pressure:
Relentless self-management: Checking sugar levels, planning meals, exercising, managing stress, taking medicines: the list never stops.
For Type 1: Lifelong insulin therapy, routine glucose checks, device maintenance, and the mental load of “keeping yourself alive” every day.
For Type 2: Regular monitoring, avoiding high-sugar foods, staying active, taking medication on time, and tracking organ health.
Fear of complications: As per the IDF report, almost 76% diabetics are concerned about side effects of diabetes on nerves, vision, heart, and kidneys.
Limited access to care: Less than 25% of Indians have health insurance, and annual diabetes care costs (~₹15,535 per person) strain many families.
Regional and income disparity: Rural states struggle with awareness and access, while urban stress and lifestyle choices push up diabetes rates.
Difficulty accessing medicines and support: Shortages, high costs, or lack of guidance can add daily frustration.
Stress and Blood Sugar: How One Affects the Other
Stress doesn’t just affect your mind, but also pushes your blood sugar in the wrong direction, too.
When you're stressed, your body releases cortisol and adrenaline, which trigger the liver to release extra glucose.
For someone with diabetes, this surge can keep blood sugar high for much longer.
Stress often disrupts routines: missing a dose, delaying meals, or skipping checks becomes more common, which only adds more stress.
Many people turn to smoking, alcohol, or other harmful substances to cope, but these habits make blood sugar control even harder.
Strained relationships, mood swings, and a dip in intimacy can show up during stressful periods, affecting overall well-being.
Stress eating is a real phenomenon, often involving high-carb comfort foods that quickly spike blood sugar levels.
Lifestyle Changes to Manage Stress and Glucose Levels
Here are a few practical suggestions that can ease stress and help you keep your blood sugar steady:
If you’re constantly irritated, tired, having trouble focusing, or feeling on edge, consulting a mental health professional can make diabetes management far more manageable.
In some cases, treating anxiety or depression with prescribed antidepressants has shown better HbA1c outcomes, as improved mood often leads to more consistent self-care.
Mindfulness tools like yoga, meditation, or slow breathing help calm the body’s stress response and gently lower cortisol levels.
A steady routine of regular, balanced meals, planned breaks, and realistic work boundaries supports better medication adherence, freeing up time for connection and rest.
Sleep for almost 7-8 hours to be ready for the next day.
Utilise tools like Continuous Glucose Monitoring devices, which offer constant, painless tracking, allowing you to act before your glucose levels dip or spike. They take a lot of guesswork and worry away from daily management.
Regular exercise of at least 150 minutes a week enhances insulin sensitivity and improves your mood. Choose what you like: walking, dancing, yoga, swimming, or light strength training.
Be joyful always. A hobby, a conversation, or even a quiet moment can refill your emotional energy and make long-term diabetes care feel sustainable.
Care for Your Mind to Care for Your Diabetes
Managing diabetes isn’t just about numbers but also about protecting your mental space. When stress stays low, blood sugar is easier to manage, and daily routines feel lighter. Staying connected with loved ones and a support group motivates you to lead a healthy life. When you think about family, having a safety net as offered by the Bajaj Life Diabetic Term Plan II Sub 8 HbA1c term plan can give you extra peace of mind while focusing on your well-being.
Key Takeaways
Stress and diabetes are closely linked; when your mind struggles, your blood sugar often follows.
Daily diabetes care makes most diabetics in India experience anxiety or depression, making mental health support essential.
Diabetes creates daily pressure: constant glucose checks, strict routines, fear of complications, and the cost of care all add to ongoing stress.
Stress hormones push blood sugar higher and can lead to missed medication, overeating, and unhealthy habits.
Use of prescribed antidepressants, tech like CGMs can help to manage both stress and glucose levels.
Daily routines, therapy, mindfulness, and regular activity can make diabetes management easier.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
What are the main types of diabetes?There are 2 types:
Type 1 diabetes requires lifelong insulin therapy because the body cannot produce it.
Type 2 happens when the body becomes resistant to insulin. Both require daily care, monitoring, and a balanced lifestyle.
How does stress affect diabetes?Stress raises cortisol and adrenaline, which increases blood sugar. It can also lead to irritability, overeating, unhealthy habits, and missing medications, ultimately making diabetes more challenging to control.
Do Continuous Glucose Monitors (CGMs) really help?Yes. CGMs track your glucose every few minutes, show trends, and alert you early. They lower anxiety, reduce guesswork, and make daily diabetes management far easier.
Why does diabetes lead to so much emotional pressure?Daily decisions, strict routines, fear of complications, and treatment costs in diabetes can feel overwhelming without the proper support and increase stress.
What are some simple steps that can help manage stress with diabetes?Eat mindfully, exercise daily, get enough sleep, seek a doctor’s guidance, and use tools like CGMs (Continuous Glucose Monitors) to help calm your mind and maintain steadier blood sugar.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.

And when diabetes feels overwhelming, it can, in turn, affect your emotional well-being. It becomes a challenging loop that can be broken with the proper support. Let’s know about the mental health challenges associated with diabetes and how to overcome them.
Why Mental Well-Being Matters in Diabetes Management?1
Let’s first understand the different types of diabetes to know how they affect the routine and emotional balance, along with blood sugar levels:
1. Type 1 diabetes happens when the immune system destroys insulin-producing cells. Since
the body can’t produce insulin, lifelong injections or an insulin pump are needed. 2. Type 2 diabetes develops when the body becomes resistant to the effects of insulin. It’s
usually linked to lifestyle and is managed with diet changes, exercise, medicines, and regular monitoring.
Both forms require daily decisions, discipline, and stress control, which directly tie into mental health. The recent survey done by the International Diabetes Federation also brought surprising results into view:2
1. 86% of diabetics report depression, anxiety, or emotional distress. 2. Women are more affected: 90% of diabetic women say their condition has impacted their
mental well-being, as against 84% of men. 3. 85% got frustrated with the everyday diabetes care routine, and 73% of them paused their
medication or treatment due to this mental fatigue.
Why Diabetes Often Leads to Stress?
Managing diabetes is constant, and here’s why many people feel overwhelmed while handling this pressure:
1. Relentless self-management: Checking sugar levels, planning meals, exercising, managing
stress, taking medicines: the list never stops. 2. For Type 1: Lifelong insulin therapy, routine glucose checks, device maintenance, and the
mental load that comes every day. 3. For Type 2: Regular monitoring, avoiding high-sugar foods, staying active, taking medication
on time, and tracking organ health. 4. Fear of complications: As per the IDF report, almost 76% diabetics are concerned about side
effects of diabetes on nerves, vision, heart, and kidneys.2 5. Limited access to care: Less than 25% of Indians are insured, and annual diabetes care costs
(~₹15,535 per person) strain many families.1
6. Regional and income disparity: Rural states struggle with awareness and access, while
urban stress and lifestyle choices push up diabetes rates. 7. Difficulty accessing medicines and support: Shortages, high costs, or lack of guidance can
add daily frustration.
Stress and Blood Sugar: How One Affects the Other
Stress doesn’t just affect your mind, but also pushes your blood sugar in the wrong direction, too.
1. When you're stressed, your body releases cortisol and adrenaline, which trigger the liver to
release extra glucose. 2. For someone with diabetes, this surge can keep blood sugar high for much longer. 3. Stress often disrupts routines: missing a dose, delaying meals, or skipping checks becomes
more common, which only adds more stress. 4. Many people turn to smoking, alcohol, or other harmful substances to cope, but these habits
make blood sugar control even harder. 5. Stress eating is a phenomenon, often involving high-carb comfort foods that quickly spike
blood sugar levels.
Lifestyle Changes to Manage Stress and Glucose Levels
Here are a few practical suggestions that can ease stress and help you keep your blood sugar steady:
1. If you’re constantly irritated, tired, having trouble focusing, or feeling on edge, consulting a
mental health professional can make diabetes management far more manageable. 2. In some cases, treating anxiety or depression with prescribed antidepressants has shown
better HbA1c outcomes, as improved mood often leads to more consistent self-care.3 3. Mindfulness tools like yoga, meditation, or slow breathing help calm the body’s stress
response and gently lower cortisol levels. 4. A steady routine of regular, balanced meals, planned breaks, and realistic work boundaries
supports better medication adherence, freeing up time for connection and rest. 5. Sleep for almost 7-8 hours to be ready for the next day. 6. Utilise tools like Continuous Glucose Monitoring devices, which offer constant, painless
tracking, allowing you to act before your glucose levels dip or spike. They take a lot of guesswork and worry away from daily management.4 7. Regular exercise of at least 150 minutes a week enhances insulin sensitivity and improves
your mood.4 Choose what you like: walking, dancing, yoga, swimming, or light strength training. 8. Be joyful always. A hobby, a conversation, or even a quiet moment can refill your emotional
energy and make long-term diabetes care feel sustainable.
Care for Your Mind to Care for Your Diabetes
Managing diabetes isn’t just about numbers but also about protecting your mental space. When stress stays low, blood sugar is easier to manage, and daily routines feel lighter. Staying connected with loved ones and a support group motivates you to lead a healthy life. When you think about
family, having a safety net as offered by the Bajaj Life Diabetic Term Plan II Sub 8 HbA1c term plan can give you extra peace of mind while focusing on your well-being.
Key Takeaways
1. Stress and diabetes are closely linked; when your mind struggles, your blood sugar often
follows. 2. Daily diabetes care makes most diabetics in India experience anxiety or depression, making
mental health support essential. 3. Diabetes creates daily pressure: constant glucose checks, strict routines, fear of
complications, and the cost of care all add to ongoing stress. 4. Stress hormones push blood sugar higher and can lead to missed medication, overeating,
and unhealthy habits. 5. Use of prescribed antidepressants, tech like CGMs can help to manage both stress and
glucose levels. 6. Daily routines, therapy, mindfulness, and regular activity can make diabetes management
easier.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
1. What are the types of diabetes?
There are 2 types:
● Type 1 diabetes requires lifelong insulin therapy because the body cannot produce it.
● Type 2 happens when the body becomes resistant to insulin. Both require daily care, monitoring, and a balanced lifestyle. 2. How does stress affect diabetes?
Stress raises cortisol and adrenaline, which increases blood sugar. It can also lead to irritability, overeating, unhealthy habits, and missing medications, ultimately making diabetes more challenging to control. 3. Do Continuous Glucose Monitors (CGMs) really help?
Yes. CGMs track your glucose every few minutes, show trends, and alert you early. They lower anxiety, reduce guesswork, and make daily diabetes management far easier. 4. Why does diabetes lead to so much emotional pressure?
Daily decisions, strict routines, fear of complications, and treatment costs in diabetes can feel overwhelming without the proper support and increase stress. 5. What are some simple steps that can help manage stress with diabetes?
Eat mindfully, exercise daily, get enough sleep, seek a doctor’s guidance, and use tools like CGMs (Continuous Glucose Monitors) to help calm your mind and maintain steadier blood sugar.
